message:
fix(planner): sort join visit order by estimated_rows instead of estimated_cost, fix qualified predicate pushdown

Two fixes to the query planner:

1. DPccp join ordering: The visit_order sort in dpccp_order_joins() was
   sorting by estimated_cost, but the cost metric conflates I/O cost
   with selectivity. For the greedy seed order that feeds into the
   dynamic-programming search, what matters is *cardinality* (how many
   rows flow into the next join). Sorting by estimated_rows ensures the
   smallest relations are visited first, which produces a better initial
   plan and prunes more of the DPccp search space. The cost is now used
   only as a tiebreaker when two tables have the same row estimate.

   This fixes the TPC-H join ordering test where region (5 rows) and
   nation (25 rows) were not consistently placed first because their
   costs happened to sort differently than their cardinalities.

2. Predicate pushdown for qualified columns: When a WHERE term had no
   table references in its expression tree (refs.is_empty()) but DID
   have a column with an explicit table qualifier (e.g., `t.col > 5`
   where the RHS is a literal), the old code fell through to the
   single-table fallback. This meant that in multi-table queries, the
   predicate would not be pushed down at all, forcing a post-join
   filter. The fix checks `col.table` first and matches it against the
   known table names (case-insensitive), pushing down to the correct
   table even when refs is empty.

Also adds diagnostic println/eprintln in the planner and harness test
to aid debugging of join order issues during development.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
